agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Insulin resistance is identified as the impaired biologic response of target tissues to insulin stimulation. All tissues with insulin receptors can become insulin resistant, but the tissues that primarily drive insulin resistance are the liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose tissue. Insulin resistance impairs glucose disposal, resulting in a compensatory increase in beta-cell insulin production and hyperinsulinemia. Recent studies have debated whether hyperinsulinemia precedes insulin resistance, as hyperinsulinemia itself is a driver of insulin resistance. This concept may be clinically valuable, suggesting that hyperinsulinemia associated with excess caloric intake may drive the metabolic dysfunction associated with insulin resistance. The metabolic consequences of insulin resistance include hyperglycemia, hypertension, dyslipidemia, hyperuricemia, elevated inflammatory markers, endothelial dysfunction, and a prothrombotic state. Progression of insulin resistance can lead to metabolic syndrome, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), and type 2 diabetes. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Clinical Significance -- Hypertension Control
agent1_snippets.content: Hypertension is one of the main comorbidities in patients with diabetes. Hypertension itself can be challenging to control in some patients, and with diabetes, it represents an even more complex relationship. [31] In these patients, hypertension is two times more prevalent than in patients who do not have diabetes and is an important component of minimizing cardiovascular risk in type 2 diabetes patients. Both hypertension and diabetes mellitus are among the top three most diagnosed health conditions in the United States. Patients with diabetes mellitus are more likely to have elevated systolic blood pressure, which is more resistant to treatment. [32] The comorbidities of hypertension and diabetes are also prevalent in obese patients. The EUROASPIRE IV survey, which included 6187 patients with coronary artery disease and hyperglycemia across 24 European countries, found that only 54% of patients with diabetes actually achieve their goal blood pressure of less than 140/90 mmHg, displaying poor blood pressure control in this population and likely in many others. [33]

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Insulin resistance, identified as an impaired biologic response to insulin stimulation of target tissues, primarily involves liver, muscle, and adipose tissue. Insulin resistance impairs glucose disposal, resulting in a compensatory increase in beta-cell insulin production and hyperinsulinemia. The metabolic consequences of insulin resistance can result in hyperglycemia, hypertension, dyslipidemia, hyperuricemia, elevated inflammatory markers, endothelial dysfunction, and a prothrombotic state. The predominant consequence of insulin resistance is type 2 diabetes(T2D). Insulin resistance is thought to precede the development of T2D by 10 to 15 years. Lifestyle modifications should be the primary focus when treating insulin resistance. Nutritional intervention with calorie reduction and avoidance of carbohydrates that stimulate excessive insulin demand is a cornerstone of treatment. Physical activity helps to increase energy expenditure and improve skeletal muscle insulin sensitivity. Medications also can improve insulin response and reduce insulin demand. Most of the complications from insulin resistance are related to the development of vascular complications and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. This activity reviews the etiology, pathogenesis, epidemiology, presentation, treatment, and potential complications of insulin resistance and highlights the crucial role of the interprofessional team in its management.

agent1_snippets.title: Plasma Glucose -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Type 2 diabetes occurs in the majority of patients with weight gain. This type is more strongly associated with obesity, progressive metabolic dysregulation, and insulin resistance. [33] The body produces insulin in type 2 diabetes, but the cells are less sensitive to insulin; hence, a higher insulin level is needed to stimulate cells to take up glucose. [34] The insulin resistance syndrome (also known as syndrome X or the metabolic syndrome) is a constellation of associated clinical and laboratory findings, consisting of insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia, obesity, dyslipidemia (high triglyceride and low high-density lipoprotein [HDL] cholesterol), and hypertension. Individuals with this syndrome are at increased risk for cardiovascular disease. [35]

agent1_snippets.content: Multiple criteria for metabolic syndrome (MetS) exist. In 2009, a joint scientific statement harmonizing criteria for MetS was released. [38] MetS is identified by the presence of 3 or more of the following diagnostic cut points: A waist circumference of 32” to 40” based on gender and ethnicity Elevated triglycerides greater than or equal to 150 mg/dL or on medication to treat hypertriglyceridemia Reduced HDL less than 40 mg/dL in males or less than 50 mg/dL in females Elevated blood pressure greater than or equal to 130 mm Hg systolic or greater than or equal to 85 mm Hg diastolic or on antihypertensive medication Elevated fasting glucose greater than or equal to 100 mg/dL or on a glucose-lowering agent The American College of Endocrinology identifies specific physiologic abnormalities that increase IRS risk. agent1_snippets.title: Metabolic Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Metabolic syndrome, characterized by a constellation of metabolic abnormalities, including central obesity, insulin resistance, hypertension, and dyslipidemia, poses a significant risk for the development of atherosclerotic cardiovascular diseases and type II diabetes mellitus. The diagnosis of metabolic syndrome necessitates the presence of 3 or more of these metabolic abnormalities, signaling an urgent need for proactive identification and intervention strategies. Alarmingly, the prevalence of metabolic syndrome has surged in recent decades, paralleling the global rise in obesity rates, with over one-fifth of Americans and Europeans currently affected.

agent1_snippets.title: Glucose Intolerance -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Glucose intolerance (GI) can be defined as dysglycemia that comprises both prediabetes and diabetes. It includes the conditions of impaired fasting glucose (IFG) and impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) and diabetes mellitus (DM). The focus of this review is on IGT and IFG. People with these conditions have an increased risk of developing diabetes and cardiovascular disease (CVD). WHO recommends using the term intermediate hyperglycemia instead of pre-diabetes to avoid the stigma associated with diabetes. Furthermore, not all cases are destined to progress to DM. In fact, the yearly conversion rate appears to range between 5% to 10% with the higher rate for IFG.

agent1_snippets.title: Prediabetes -- Pathophysiology
agent1_snippets.content: Since prediabetes is the precursor for diabetes mellitus, the pathophysiology is relatable. Hyperglycemia will cause production and release of insulin by the pancreatic beta cells. Excess insulin exposure for long periods of time diminishes the reponse of the insulin receptors the function of which is to open glucose channels leading to entry of glucose into the cells. Decreased function of the insulin receptors leads to further hyperglycemia further perpetuating the metabolic disturbance and leading to the development of not only diabetes type 2 but also metabolic syndrome. In prediabetes, this process is not to the extent of diabetes mellitus but is a first step in a metabolic cascade which has potentially dangerous consequences if not adequately addressed. Hence its imperative to start treatment at the earliest. [7] If treatment is not started or if the treatment is not adequate, adverse effects on large and small blood vessels (e.g. arteries of the cardiovascular system or retina, kidney, and nerves) may occure.

agent1_snippets.title: Metabolic Syndrome -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Metabolic syndrome is an accumulation of several disorders that raise the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, including myocardial infarction, cerebrovascular accidents, peripheral vascular diseases, insulin resistance, and type II diabetes mellitus. The cluster of metabolic disorders that define metabolic syndrome includes central obesity, insulin resistance, hypertension, and atherogenic dyslipidemia. [1] The diagnosis of metabolic syndrome requires the presence of 3 or more metabolic abnormalities: A waist circumference of more than 40 inches in men and 35 inches in women Serum triglycerides level of 150 mg/dL or greater Reduced high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, less than 40 mg/dL in men or less than 50 mg/dL in women Elevated fasting glucose of l00 mg/dL or greater Blood pressure values of systolic 130 mm Hg or higher or diastolic 85 mm Hg or higher [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Candidates for Therapy
agent1_snippets.content: GLP-1RAs are appropriate for patients with existing atherosclerotic disease when weight loss is a primary goal and/or avoidance of hypoglycemia is a primary concern. Although GLP-1RAs can be used as an adjunct therapy in most patients, they should not be used with dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitors due to little additional benefit from this combined therapy or with prandial insulin due to limited data to support their use. [28] GLP-1RA has been shown to improve glycemic control and reduce insulin injection dependence when used with basal insulin. [29] GLP-1RAs should not be used in patients with type 1 diabetes or those with an estimated eGFR of less than 30 mL/min/1.73 m2.

agent1_snippets.title: Metabolic Syndrome -- Etiology
agent1_snippets.content: Insulin resistance can be acquired or due to genetic predisposition. Impairment of the signaling pathway, insulin receptor defects, and defective insulin secretion can all contribute to insulin resistance. Visceral obesity has been identified as the main trigger of all pathways involved in the pathogenesis of metabolic syndrome, and high-calorie intake is the primary cause of visceral fat accumulation. [8] Over time, the culmination causes the development of metabolic syndrome that presents as vascular and autonomic damage. [15]

agent1_snippets.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
agent1_snippets.content: Data and evidence from large observational studies have shown a positive association between glycemia measurements, including hemoglobin A1C and fasting glucose levels, with the risk associated with cardiovascular disease. Specifically, these studies have shown that hyperglycemia is a modifiable risk factor for coronary artery disease and atherosclerotic disease. In a meta-analysis of 10 independent studies involving the measurement of hemoglobin A1C in individuals with diabetes and cardiovascular disease, it was discovered that the relative risk for total cardiovascular disease and individuals with type 2 diabetes was 1.18 for each one percent increase in glycosylated hemoglobin, indicating an increase cardiovascular risk with increasing hyperglycemia. [9]

agent1_snippets.title: Appropriate Use of SGLT2s and GLP-1 RAs With Insulin to Reduce CVD Risk in Patients With Diabetes (Archived) -- Other Issues -- Use of SGLT2 Inhibitors and GLP-1RAs with Insulin in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
agent1_snippets.content: As stated above by the ADA guidelines, insulin is effective and preferred in patients with catabolic diabetes. In the absence of catabolic symptoms (weight loss or hemoglobin A1C greater than 10%), GLP-1RAs are an alternative to insulin. When hemoglobin A1C is markedly elevated but less than 10%, both basal insulin or a GLP-1RA can be used as add-on therapy to metformin, as they have been shown to have good glycemic efficacy in these patients. [49] GLP-1RAs have shown at-least comparable glycemic efficacy when compared to basal insulin with the added benefit of weight loss which is usually desirable in most of these patients. [49] For patients with moderately elevated hemoglobin A1C levels and established cardiac or renal disease, SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1RA should be added to metformin therapy before adding insulin or other agents due to their documented efficacy in improving cardiovascular mortality and slowing the progressive renal disease. In patients with a risk of hypoglycemia, GLP-1RA, SGLT2 inhibitors, and dipeptidyl peptidase 4 (DPP-4) inhibitors are the treatment of choice as they do not increase the risk of hypoglycemia as opposed to insulin.

agent1_snippets.title: Insulin Resistance -- Pearls and Other Issues
agent1_snippets.content: Intensive lifestyle intervention should be the first line of therapy for patients with metabolic syndrome or insulin resistance syndrome. The benefits of exercise cannot be understated in treating patients with insulin resistance. Barriers to exercise should be discussed, and a well-formulated plan, including moderate-intensity cardiovascular exercise like walking, should be provided in accordance with the physical activity guidelines. Discussion of dietary modification following the dietary guidelines should also be provided with individualization to the patient's preferences, with particular attention to reducing sugar, refined grain products, and high glycemic index carbohydrates.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756
agent2_snippets.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.

agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several related disorders: ارتفاع السكر في الدم (elevated blood glucose), مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). These entities represent a spectrum of pancreatic endocrine dysfunction, ranging from simple hyperglycemia to specific complications such as neuropathy, as well as hormonal excess states like hyperinsulinemia and glucagon‑secreting tumors. Understanding that all of these conditions are subtypes of diseases of the endocrine pancreas, including diabetes, helps clinicians recognize the varied presentations, anticipate potential complications, and tailor comprehensive patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
agent2_snippets.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1545
agent2_snippets.content: The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of pancreatic and metabolic disorders. Chronic complications of diabetes are reflected in “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), a progressive nerve damage that can cause pain, sensory loss, and autonomic dysfunction. Hormonal dysregulation associated with pancreatic endocrine tumors is represented by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), often due to insulin‑secreting neoplasms, and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma), a rare tumor that overproduces glucagon leading to characteristic skin and metabolic findings. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that encompasses several specific conditions. Among its subentities are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), a chronic complication in which prolonged hyperglycemia leads to damage of peripheral nerves and results in sensory loss, pain, or autonomic dysfunction. The category also includes فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), a state of elevated insulin levels in the bloodstream that can arise from insulin resistance, early type 2 diabetes, or insulin‑secreting tumors and contributes to metabolic disturbances such as hypoglycemia and weight gain. Another child condition is ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma), a rare neoplasm of the pancreatic α‑cells that secretes excess glucagon, producing a characteristic syndrome of hyperglycemia, necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, and anemia. Finally, the category lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), an uncommon, often autoimmune‑mediated disorder characterized by gradual wasting of the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle on one side of the face; its inclusion reflects the recognition that endocrine pancreatic diseases, particularly those with autoimmune components, may be associated with extra‑pancreatic manifestations. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical spectrum—from metabolic and neuropathic complications to neoplastic and autoimmune manifestations—linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and multidisciplinary management in affected patients.

agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is classified as a subgroup of other endocrine disorders. Within this pancreatic endocrine disease category are several specific conditions: ارتفاع السكر في الدم (hyperglycemia or elevated blood glucose), نقص تركيز السكر (hypoglycemia or low blood glucose), اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia or excess insulin in the blood), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma, a tumor that secretes excess glucagon). These entities together represent the spectrum of metabolic disturbances, chronic complications, and neoplastic processes that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine cells. Hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia illustrate opposite dysregulations of glucose homeostasis, while hyperinsulinemia reflects overproduction of insulin and glucagonoma reflects overproduction of glucagon. Diabetic neuropathy is a long‑term complication that develops from persistent hyperglycemia. All of these conditions are linked under the broader umbrella of other endocrine disorders, highlighting their common basis in hormone imbalance. Although one relationship suggested that نقص تركيز السكر might be a parent of other endocrine disorders, the more coherent hierarchy places DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES as the child of other endocrine disorders, with نقص تركيز السكر correctly understood as one of its constituent manifestations.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
agent2_snippets.content: Dipeptidyl peptidase 4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors for blood glucose lowering constitute a therapeutic class of oral antihyperglycemic agents that improve glycemic control by prolonging the activity of incretin hormones. Within this class, alogliptin and sitagliptin are recognized as individual members, each approved for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus. Although simvastatin is a lipid‑lowering statin and not itself a DPP‑4 inhibitor, it is sometimes combined with sitagliptin in a fixed‑dose formulation to address both hyperglycemia and hypercholesterolemia in patients who require concurrent management of diabetes and dyslipidemia. This integrated approach reflects the clinical practice of tailoring pharmacotherapy to target multiple metabolic risk factors simultaneously.
